frameworks.name: population codebook / maximum-entropy population coding
frameworks.one_line: vocabulary of binary spike patterns across simultaneously recorded units, shaped by pairwise correlations

frameworks.name: independent (rate-only) population model
frameworks.one_line: baseline that ignores spatial and temporal correlations between units

frameworks.name: Markov / history-dependent sequence dynamics
frameworks.one_line: transitions between population patterns with epoch-specific correlation time scales

frameworks.name: encoding/decoding asymmetry
frameworks.one_line: complex encoder paired with simple downstream readout

core_question: How do spatial and temporal spiking correlations shape the population code of prefrontal cortex across distinct task epochs, and does that complexity matter for decoding behavior?
key_assumptions: 10 ms time bins capture relevant fine temporal structure
population activity can be described as a vocabulary of binary patterns (codebook)
task epochs index distinct computational regimes
decoders are evaluated on behavioral readout from population responses
manipulations: task epoch within a visual discrimination task
model class: correlated vs rate-only (independent) population models
key_findings: different codebooks of population patterns are used in different task epochs
spiking correlations are required to capture the observed codebooks; rate-only models fail
pattern sequences are strongly history-dependent with epoch-specific transition probabilities and correlation time scales
observed sequences are many orders of magnitude more likely than predicted by correlation-ignoring models, yet simple correlation-ignoring decoders read out behavior well
interpretations: PFC uses an encoding scheme whose spatiotemporal complexity is structured to permit simple downstream decoders that need not learn correlations
